Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/windsurf-next.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-3t3v402w...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array that fetches a `.deb` file from a known apt repository URL and two local desktop files. The URL is constructed using predefined variables (`_apt_base`, `_upstream_ver`, `_debfile`), but does not involve command substitution or dynamic code execution during sourcing. The `source` array entries are static in structure and refer to a legitimate upstream asset (the `.deb` package), which is expected for a packaging workflow.

The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic version computation occurs. All variable assignments are simple string manipulations or constant values. No code in the global scope performs network operations, file modifications, or execution of external payloads. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions contain logic to extract and install the deb, but these are not executed due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. Therefore, sourcing this PKGBUILD for `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe and poses no risk of unintended code execution or data exfiltration.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: No malicious execution during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: No malicious execution during source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, windsurf-next-url-handler.desktop...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a .deb file from a remote APT repository hosted at `https://windsurf-stable.codeiumdata.com/mQfcApCOdSLoWOSI/apt`, which is an obfuscated and highly suspicious URL. The path segment `mQfcApCOdSLoWOSI` appears to be a randomly generated string, indicating potential use of a third-party hosting service with opaque access controls, not a standard upstream distribution mechanism. This deviates from typical AUR packaging practices where sources are either directly from official upstreams or well-documented mirrors.

While downloading a .deb from an upstream is not inherently unsafe, the domain `codeiumdata.com` is associated with Codeium — a different company — raising red flags about trust boundaries and potential confusion of ownership. More critically, the package is not being built from source but is instead repackaging a pre-compiled binary distributed in an opaque manner, with no verification of the origin or integrity beyond the embedded checksum. This creates a supply-chain risk, especially since the update is auto-generated via GitHub Actions, increasing the likelihood of automated compromise or unnoticed source substitution.

Although the checksums are present and the rest of the build process appears normal (extracting the .deb, copying files, setting symlinks), the reliance on a non-upstream, obscurely structured repository for a proprietary binary without transparency into build provenance constitutes a substantial security risk. It prevents auditability and enables potential stealth injection of malicious payloads into the binary without the maintainer or users being aware.
</details>
<summary>
Suspicious binary source from obfuscated third-party domain
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing windsurf-next-url-handler.desktop...
x Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: UNSAFE -- Suspicious binary source from obfuscated third-party domain
LLM auditresponse for windsurf-next-url-handler.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.desktop` file is a standard desktop entry configuration used to register a URL handler for the windsurf-next application. It defines metadata such as the application name, icon, executable path, MIME types, and categories, all of which are typical for desktop integration on Linux systems.

The `Exec` line correctly points to the application binary under `/opt/windsurf-next/`, which is a standard install location for third-party software. The `MimeType` field registers custom URI schemes (`dever-next`, `windsurf-next`), which is expected behavior for applications that need to handle custom URL protocols. There are no network operations, file manipulations, or execution of arbitrary code present in this file.

No obfuscation, dangerous commands, or suspicious behaviors are found. The content aligns with standard packaging and desktop integration practices for AUR packages. While using `/opt` may raise minor hygiene considerations if not properly secured, the file itself does not introduce any malicious logic.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop file, no malicious content detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

Reviewed all required files.
Required file review did not complete cleanly; skipping additional review.

While downloading a .deb from an upstream is not inherently unsafe, the domain `codeiumdata.com` is associated with Codeium — a different company — raising red flags about trust boundaries and potential confusion of ownership. More critically, the package is not being built from source but is instead repackaging a pre-compiled binary distributed in an opaque manner, with no verification of the origin or integrity beyond the embedded checksum. Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only declares variables and arrays at the top-level scope. No dangerous commands (curl, wget, eval, base64 decode, etc.) are present outside of `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` — which are **not executed** during the `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` step. All sources are fetched over HTTPS from the expected upstream APT repository (`windsurf-stable.codeiumdata.com`) with a pinned SHA256 checksum, and the two `.desktop` files are local to the package. No `pkgver()` function exists to execute code at source time. Fetching these sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is therefore safe.
</details>
<summary>No top-level malicious code; sources are standard and pinned.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD follows standard Arch Linux packaging practices. It downloads a `.deb` file from the official upstream APT repository (`windsurf-stable.codeiumdata.com`) via HTTPS with pinned SHA-256 checksums. The `prepare()` function extracts the `.deb` archive and its data tarball using `ar` and `tar`, which is standard for repackaging Debian packages. The `package()` function installs files to `/opt/$pkgname`, creates symlinks in `/usr/bin`, installs desktop files, completions, and icons, and sets appropriate permissions (including `chmod 4755` on `chrome-sandbox` which is a standard SUID requirement for Electron/Chromium-based sandboxing). No evidence of obfuscated code, unexpected network requests, or data exfiltration. The file is consistent with legitimate AUR packaging for a binary application from its official source.  
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR PKGBUILD for repackaging a binary from upstream.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for askpass.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This script is a standard VSCode Git credential helper it uses a temporary pipe (`mktemp`) to securely pass the Git askpass response from a Node.js process back to the shell. It sets internal VSCode environment variables and executes the VSCode-supplied Node.js binary and main script. The final `cat` and `rm` operations read and clean up the pipe, which is normal behavior for this pattern. There is no evidence of malicious activity such as data exfiltration, code injection, or unexpected network calls. The script performs exactly its stated purpose: handling Git HTTPS authentication prompts for VSCode.
</details>
<summary>Standard VSCode Git credential helper script, no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

All variables are expected to be set by the upstream Windsurf application. There are no network requests, encoded commands, unusual file operations, or any behavior that deviates from supporting the application's intended functionality. LLM auditresponse for devin-desktop-next.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `devin-desktop-next.desktop` is a standard `.desktop` entry file for a Linux desktop application, intended to be installed system-wide for menu/launcher integration. It defines a text editor/IDE named "Devin - Next" that launches the binary `/usr/share/devin-desktop-next/devin-desktop-next` with optional file arguments (`%F`). There is also a desktop action for opening a new empty window, which invokes the same binary with `--new-window`.  

The content contains no obfuscated code, no network requests, no downloads, no encoded commands, no file modifications, and no system configuration changes. It is a simple static configuration file that follows standard freedesktop.org desktop entry specifications. The `Exec` lines reference a path within the package's own installation directory, which is normal practice.  

No supply-chain attack indicators are present. The file is safe.
</details>
<summary>Standard .desktop file, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for git-editor-main.js:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is the compiled/minified main script for the Git editor component of the Windsurf extension (a VS Code derivative). It uses `http.request` with a Unix socket path (from `VSCODE_GIT_IPC_HANDLE`) to communicate with the parent process, sending the last command-line argument (the commit message path) to the `git-editor` IPC handler. This is standard VS Code IPC behavior and does not involve any external network requests, obfuscated code, or unexpected system modifications. The source map URL pointing to `cdn.windsurf.com` is the project's own CDN and is expected. No evidence of malicious or dangerous behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard Git extension IPC script, no malicious behavior.</summary>

This script is a standard SSH askpass helper for VS Code (and forks like Windsurf). It creates a temporary named pipe, then invokes the VS Code Git askpass Node.js helper application with appropriate environment variables (`ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE=1`, `VSCODE_GIT_ASKPASS_TYPE=ssh`, and the pipe path). The Node helper is expected to write the SSH passphrase into the pipe, which the script then reads with `cat` and removes. No network requests, obfuscated code, unexpected system modifications, or data exfiltration occur. The environment variables are set by the host application (VS Code) and are consistent with normal upstream behavior. There is no evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious intent.

No issues found.

data.reasoning: The PKGBUILD downloads a .deb from windsurf-stable.codeiumdata.com, which is the legitimate official distribution host for Windsurf (formerly Codeium). The random-looking path segment is a standard pattern for vendor APT repositories (and is consistent across the official upstream). Sources are pinned with SHA256 checksums and fetched over HTTPS. The prepare()/package() functions perform standard .deb extraction and installation with no malicious commands. The deepseek model reviewed 12 files including all shell scripts and key extension JS files, finding only standard VS Code fork behavior. The qwen model's UNSAFE verdict is a false positive based on misunderstanding the official upstream domain and conflating 'proprietary closed-source binary repackaging' with malice.
data.disagreement_analysis: qwen3 flagged the package UNSAFE solely on the basis that the source URL contains a random-looking path segment and the codeiumdata.com domain, interpreting these as 'obfuscation' and a trust-boundary red flag. This is a misjudgment: codeiumdata.com is the legitimate Codeium/Windsurf distribution domain, and tokenized APT repo paths are normal. qwen also only reviewed 2 files and never inspected the extracted contents, while deepseek performed a thorough 12-file review and correctly identified all content as standard VS Code fork artifacts.
coverage_issues: qwen3 reviewed only 2 files (PKGBUILD + one .desktop) and skipped all extracted source/scripts, aborting additional review after marking PKGBUILD unsafe.
Neither model can verify the contents of the prebuilt binary itself (inherent limitation of binary repackaging), but this is not a package-specific defect.
false_positives: qwen3: 'Suspicious binary source from obfuscated third-party domain' — codeiumdata.com is the official Windsurf/Codeium distribution host and the tokenized path is a normal vendor APT repo structure, not obfuscation.

qwen3: 'supply-chain risk because auto-generated via GitHub Actions and proprietary binary' — repackaging an official upstream binary with pinned checksums over HTTPS is standard and accepted AUR practice (cf. many -bin packages).
learnings: Models should be instructed that vendor distribution domains with tokenized/random-looking path segments are common and NOT inherently 'obfuscation'; verdicts should not hinge on URL aesthetics alone.

Repackaging an official upstream prebuilt binary (.deb) with pinned SHA256 checksums over HTTPS is normal AUR practice and should not by itself constitute an UNSAFE verdict — distinguish 'closed-source / unverifiable' from 'malicious'.
When a model marks a required file UNSAFE early it aborts further review (qwen did 2 files); the tool should still complete content review to avoid premature, under-informed verdicts.
Cross-model token/cost disparity is telling: qwen used 10k tokens / 2 files vs deepseek 444k / 12 files — extremely shallow audits should be weighted lower in adjudication.
Add a known-good vendor domain allowlist or context hint (e.g., codeiumdata.com = Windsurf upstream) to reduce trust-boundary false positives.
